source_metadata.title_raw: Krishan Chopra - A-19-214 - January 14, 2020 - Mountain View

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Krishan Chopra 
Mountain View City Attorney 
500 Castro Street 
P.O. Box 7540 
Mountain View, CA 94039-7540 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-214 
Dear Mr. Chopra: 
January 14, 2020 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the City of Mountain View City 
Councilmember Ellen Kamei regarding Government Code Section 1090, et seq. 1 Please note that 
we are only providing advice under Section 1090 and not under any other conflict of interest laws. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC 
Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the 
case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional 
advice. 
We are required to forward your request regarding Section 1090 and all pertinent facts 
relating to it to the Attorney General's Office and the Santa Clara County District Attorney's 
Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We are also required to specify whether we 
have received a written response from either entity: we have not. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) 
Furthermore, we are required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice 
"is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." 
(Section 1097. l(c)(S).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of Mountain View from entering into a Master License 
Agreement ("MLA") for small cell wireless installations in the City with AT&T, a large 
international telecommunications company, given that AT&T employs Councilmember Kamei as 
its External Affairs Manager? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Because the Mountain View City Council is not required to review or approve the MLA 
under City Council Policy A-10, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering into the 
1 All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-19-214 
Page No. 2 
MLA so long as the Councilmember completely refrains from participating in making the MLA in 
her official capacity. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
You are the Mountain View City Attorney and the authorized representative of Mountain 
View City Councilmember Ellen Kamei. The Mountain View City Council will soon consider a 
Master License Agreement ("MLA") for small cell wireless installations within the City, which 
could include the deployment of new 5G cellular network transmission facilities at multiple 
locations within the City's public right-of-way. 
AT&T, a large international telecommunications company, recently hired the 
Councilmember as its External Affairs Manager. In this position, the Councilmember'sjob duties 
include: 
• 
Advocating on behalf of AT&T; 
• 
Establishing and maintaining relationships with local elected officials and key stakeholders to 
support AT&T objectives; 
• 
Collaborating on and contributing to state legislative affairs efforts and policy objectives; 
• 
Collaborating on communicating with internal business units regarding the impact of pending 
local ordinances and legislation; 
• 
Promoting AT&T's policy agenda during public hearings and before third-party organizations 
and key audiences within the region; 
• 
Serving as a spokesperson for AT&T at public hearings and community events. 
The Councilmember receives a salary from AT&T as compensation for her work on its 
behalf and is eligible for an annual bonus from AT&T based on the AT&T's overall business 
performance in the prior year. The Councilmember does not hold any stock in AT&T in her role as 
External Affairs Manager. You state that the Councilmember will not conduct any work with or in 
the City on behalf of AT&T, and her salary, bonus, employee benefits, or status as an AT&T 
employee are not tied to AT&T's agreements with the City or any other local government agency. 
The City Council by resolution delegates contracting authority to various City employees 
and departments. Initially adopted by City Council resolution in 1976, and thereafter amended and 
updated by subsequent City Council resolutions over the years, City Council Policy A-10 sets forth 
the City's "schedule of authorization to execute City contracts and agreements." The purpose of 
City Council Policy A-10 is to "establish a general policy for the authorization to execute City 
contracts and agreements and increase certain limited appropriations related to grants, donations, 
and reimbursed expenditures." 


File No. A-19-214 
Page No. 3 
City Council Policy A-lO's schedule of authorization includes 18 categories of contracts and 
agreements commonly executed by the City, and you state that the MLA would be considered a 
"Contract Required as a Condition of City Approval" under Category 9 of the schedule. A 
Category-9 contract does not require City Council input or approval, and is instead evaluated or 
approved by either the City's Public Works Director if the total compensation to the City under the 
contract is less than $20,000, or by the City Manager if the total compensation to the City under the 
contract exceeds $20,000. In any case, because the MLA is a Category-9 contract under City 
Council Policy A-lO's schedule of authorization, either the Public Works Director or the City 
Manager would review or approve the MLA rather than the City Council. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 generally prohibits a public officer or employee, while acting in his or her 
official capacity, from making or participating in the making of a contract in which the officer or 
employee is financially interested. Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at actual 
impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety" ( City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey 
(1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197), and a contract made in violation of Section 1090 is void 
(Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646). 
The California Supreme Court has stated that the purpose of Section 1090 is to make certain 
that "every public officer be guided solely by the public interest, rather than by personal interest, 
when dealing with contracts in an official capacity." (Id. at p. 650.) Eliminating temptation for 
public officers and employees, obtaining their undivided loyalty, and avoiding the perception of 
impropriety are important public policy goals in California. (Id. at p. 658.) Consequently, Section 
1090 applies without regard to whether actual fraud or dishonesty is involved in the contracting 
process, the contract is fair to the public agency, or the public agency loses money from the 
contract. (Id. at pp. 648-649.) 
The Councilmember is subject to Section 1090 because Section 1090 applies to city 
councilmembers. Decisions relating to the MLA involve that contract. Because a public officer 
generally has a financial interest in a contract between his or her public agency and his or her 
private employer for purposes of Section 1090 (89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 278, 280 (2006); 88 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 108-110 (2005); 58 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 670, 677-678 (1975)), the 
Councilmember is financially interested in the potential MLA between the City and AT&T. 
We now consider whether the Councilmember would make or participate in the making of 
the potential MLA between the City and AT&T for purposes of Section 1090. Under Section 1090, 
a member of a public agency's governing body is conclusively presumed to participate in the 
making of a contract under the governing body's authority, irrespective of whether the member 
actually participates in the making of that contract. (Id. at pp. 649-650.) Therefore, Section 1090 
prohibits the entire body from entering into a contract in which a member of the body is financially 
interested, even if that member abstains from participating in the making of, or fully discloses his or 
her interest in, the contract. (Ibid.) In other words, where Section 1090 applies to a member of the 
governing body with respect to a contract, it prohibits the entire governing body from entering into 
the contract. 


File No. A-19-214 
Page No. 4 
Section 1090's prohibition, however, does not necessarily apply when an independent 
entity, such as a statutorily independent officer or a separate executive body, contracts on behalf of 
a local government agency. (81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 274, 277-278 (1998) [Section 1090 did not 
prohibit the housing authority from contracting with a county supervisor to serve as the county 
housing authority's secretary and executive director because the housing authority was sufficiently 
independent from the county]; 57 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 458 (1974) [county supervisor not prohibited 
from selling supplies to the county because county purchasing agent was an independent officer]; 
21 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 90 (1953) [city treasurer not prohibited from depositing city funds in a bank 
in which a city councilmember was financially interested because the treasurer was an independent 
officer].)2 
Section 34851 authorizes a city to establish a city manager form of government by 
ordinance, and Section 34852 requires the ordinance to "define the powers and duties of the city 
manager." The City has adopted a city manager form of government. Section 602 of Article VI of 
the Mountain View City Charter sets forth the City Manager's powers and duties, and provides, 
among other things, that the City Manager: is the head of the administrative branch of the City 
government; has the power to appoint, discipline, and remove all City officers and employees 
except as otherwise provided by the Charter; and is required to prepare rules and regulations 
governing the City's contracting for all supplies, materials, and equipment required by the City 
government. 
Section 607 of Article VI of the Mountain View City Charter prohibits the City Council or 
any of its members from interfering with the City Manager's execution of his or her powers and 
duties, requires the City Council or its members to deal with the administrative service solely 
through the City Manager ( except for purposes of inquiry), and prohibits the City Council or its 
members from giving orders to any subordinate of the City Manager. 
The City Council by resolution delegates contracting authority to various City employees 
and departments. The City Council adopted City Council Policy A-10 by resolution, and that 
Policy's purpose is to "establish a general policy for the authorization to execute City contracts and 
agreements and increase certain limited appropriations related to grants, donations, and reimbursed 
expenditures." City Council Policy A-10 sets forth the "schedule of authorization to execute City 
contracts and agreements," and that schedule includes 18 categories of contracts and agreements 
commonly executed by the City. 
You state that the MLA would be considered "Contracts Required as a Condition of City 
Approval" under Category 9 of City Council Policy A-10. As a Category-9 contract, the MLA does 
not require City Council input or approval; instead, the MLA would be evaluated or approved by 
2 See also Black Advice Letter, No. A-19-103 [Section l 090 did not prohibit a city manager from contracting 
on behalf of the city with a nonprofit organization that employed a city councilmember because the city manager was 
an independent officer]; Wilson Advice Letter, No. A-16-269 [city department head serving under city manager not 
prohibited from entering into a contract with a nonprofit organization that employed a city councilmember because the 
department head was an independent officer]; Houston Advice Letter, No. A-16-07 5 [ city councilmember not 
prohibited from pursuing public employment with a joint powers authority ("JPA") which includes the city because the 
JPA was sufficiently independent from the city]; Walter Advice Letter, No. A-15-050 [ city manager authorized to 
contract with a professional services provider despite a city councilmember being financially interested in that contract 
because city manager was an independent officer]. 


File No. A-19-214 
Page No. 5 
the City's Public Works Director if the City's total compensation from AT&T under the MLA is 
less than $20,000, or the City Manager if the City's total compensation exceeds $20,000. In any 
case, because the MLA is a Category-9 contract under City Council Policy A-10, either the Public 
Works Director or the City Manager would review or approve the MLA rather than the City 
Council. 
The City is a charter city with a city manager form of government, and the Mountain View 
City Charter sets forth the powers and duties of the City Manager and grants the City Manager 
substantial independence from the City Council. As noted above, the City Charter provides that: (I) 
the City Manager is the is the head of the City's administrative service and has the power to 
appoint, discipline, or remove all City officers or employees ( except as otherwise provided by the 
Charter); and (2) the City Council and its members are required to deal with the City's 
administrative service solely through the City Manager ( except for purposes of inquiry) and are 
prohibited from giving orders to any subordinate of the City Manager. (Mountain View City 
Charter, Sections 602 and 607). Therefore, the City Manager and the Director of Public Works are 
statutorily independent officers from the City Council for purposes of Section 1090 with respect to 
the MLA. Because the City Council is not required to review or approve the MLA under City 
Council Policy A-10, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering into the MLA so long 
as the Councilmember completely refrains from participating in the making of the MLA in her 
official capacity. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
MFC:sal 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
~¾Jr 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of Mountain View from entering into a Master License 
Agreement ("MLA") for small cell wireless installations in the City with AT&T, a large 
international telecommunications company, given that AT&T employs Councilmember Kamei as 
its External Affairs Manager?
sections.conclusion:
No. Because the Mountain View City Council is not required to review or approve the MLA 
under City Council Policy A-10, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering into the 
1 MLA so long as the Councilmember completely refrains from participating in making the MLA in 
her official capacity.

Agreement ("MLA") for small cell wireless installations in the City with AT&T, a large 
international telecommunications company, given that AT&T employs Councilmember Kamei as 
its External Affairs Manager?

CONCLUSION: No. Because the Mountain View City Council is not required to review or approve the MLA 
under City Council Policy A-10, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering into the 
1 MLA so long as the Councilmember completely refrains from participating in making the MLA in 
her official capacity.
